metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the canonical normalization rule explicitly (e.g., UTF-8 encode, strip leading/trailing whitespace per field, sort comments by ID ascending, then SHA-256) or reference the exact existing helper the new content-hash function must match.
findings.finding: The content-hash normalization rule is stated as 'title + description + comments, normalized' but 'normalized' is left undefined — the executor must guess the canonical form (case folding, whitespace stripping, comment ordering, encoding). This is a defaultable gap the plan should resolve rather than defer.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Content-hash helper over the exact fields fed to enrichment (title + description + comments, normalized)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one line to the Scope bullet: 'Normalization: UTF-8, strip leading/trailing whitespace from each field, comments sorted by creation timestamp ascending — identical to the input serialization used in [existing enrichment call site].'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the storage structure for the content-hash-keyed digest: either (a) define the map as a single-slot record `{content_hash, digest_body}` (store the hash alongside the digest but keep one slot, overwriting unconditionally on each enrichment — matching the true AC5 'latest-wins' semantics), or (b) if a multi-slot map is intended, specify the GC/eviction policy that prevents unbounded accumulation of stale entries.
findings.finding: Content-hash keying allows unbounded accumulation of stale digest entries: each distinct content-hash becomes its own map key, so a ticket that cycles through multiple content states (A → B → A) accumulates entries that are never removed, unlike the attestations map which uses a fixed kind-key with natural LWW eviction.
findings.location: Scope section / AC1 ('keyed by content-hash') / AC5 ('overwrites the prior digest … rather than accumulating')
findings.suggested_fix: Use a single fixed state key (e.g. `digest`) holding a record `{content_hash: <hash>, body: <digest>, written_at: <ts>}`. Freshness on read: compare `state['digest']['content_hash']` to the current content-hash. This achieves identical self-invalidation semantics with no accumulation risk and simpler reducer logic — one LWW slot, not a growing map.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact normalization contract for the content-hash helper: which fields are included (e.g. comment body text only vs full record), their ordering, encoding, and canonical serialization (e.g. `json.dumps(sort_keys=True)` or concatenated UTF-8 strings).
findings.finding: The content-hash normalization rule is underspecified. The plan says 'title + description + comments, normalized' but does not define what 'normalized' means for comments — whether it hashes the comment body text only, the full comment record (including author/timestamp), a sorted concatenation, or some other form. Because the stored hash is compared against a recomputed hash on every read to determine freshness, any ambiguity in the hash input means two implementations of the helper may silently diverge, causing permanent false-stale or false-fresh reads.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Content-hash helper over the exact fields fed to enrichment (title + description + comments, normalized)'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: hash = SHA-256(json.dumps({'title': title, 'description': description, 'comments': [c['body'] for c in comments]}, sort_keys=True, ensure_ascii=False)) — co-locate this contract in the plan's Scope section alongside the helper description.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py` (KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES / _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES) and `src/rebar/reducer/_replay.py` (_EVENT_HANDLERS) to the plan's edit-target list, and clarify whether the new event type is reducer-applied (KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES + _EVENT_HANDLERS) or reducer-ignored (_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES only).
findings.finding: `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py` (`KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` and/or `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES`) is an unenumerated edit target. The plan asserts older clients will preserve-and-ignore the new event — but the mechanism for that is exactly membership/non-membership in these sets, and the file is not named. Additionally, `src/rebar/reducer/_replay.py`'s `_EVENT_HANDLERS` dispatch table must be updated for the new type to actually be projected; neither file is mentioned.
findings.location: Scope section — 'New derived-record event type (preserved-and-ignored by older clients, per the rollout convention) + reducer projection into ticket state'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the content-hash keying scheme with AC5's 'latest-wins / no accumulation' requirement by explicitly specifying whether the reducer retains only the single most-recent digest entry (replacing any prior key) or by clarifying that 'accumulation' refers only to same-content idempotency and staleness detection handles multi-entry coexistence.
findings.finding: The content-hash keying scheme and the 'latest-wins / no accumulation' requirement in AC5 are in tension. Keying by content-hash means each distinct version of ticket text produces a distinct key; as ticket content drifts, old-hash entries remain alongside the new one, which is accumulation. AC5 says 'rather than accumulating,' but the described mechanism does not remove or supersede entries at differing keys.
findings.location: AC5 vs 'What' / 'Why' sections
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to 'What' or 'Scope' clarifying the single-slot rule: the reducer projects exactly one digest record per ticket (not one per content-hash version), storing both the digest payload and its content-hash together under a fixed key (e.g. 'digest'). Freshness is then derived on read by comparing the stored content-hash against the ticket's current content-hash. This resolves the contradiction and aligns with how attestations use a kind-keyed (fixed-key) map rather than a value-keyed accumulating map.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate whether a new manifest kind (e.g., `'ticket-digest'`) filed via the existing `SIGNATURE` event into `state['attestations']` satisfies all five ACs — and if a new event type is still preferred, explicitly justify the divergence (e.g., 'digest is not HMAC-verified, so the SIGNATURE event's signing contract would be misleading').
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new event type and a new top-level derived-record structure when the existing SIGNATURE event + kind-keyed attestations map already provides: kind-keying, per-kind last-writer-wins, freshness-on-read, HMAC-optional signing, compaction survival, public-state stripping, and forward-compat — all without adding a new event type. The plan does not evaluate whether a new manifest kind (e.g., 'ticket-digest') filed under attestations['ticket-digest'] via a SIGNATURE event would satisfy all five ACs, nor does it justify the divergence from the established pattern.
findings.location: ## What / ## Scope (new derived-record event type + reducer projection)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Alternatives considered' note: 'Considered reusing SIGNATURE + attestations["ticket-digest"] — rejected because [reason, e.g., the SIGNATURE event implies HMAC integrity which we do not need/want here, OR accepted because the lighter new event type avoids the signing ceremony]'. If the SIGNATURE path IS the right choice, remove the new event type from scope and route through process_signature with a new manifest kind.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-rollback schema state assertion to the plan: describe what happens to tickets that already have a TICKET_DIGEST event (and its projection in compiled_state) when the feature is rolled back, confirm older-binary SNAPSHOT compaction carries the 'digest' key forward harmlessly, and add a back-out path (e.g. a compensating 'forget-digest' event or a compaction strip step) if the field must ever be purged.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that older clients preserve-and-ignore the new event type (AC4) but never specifies the schema STATE AFTER rollback. Per the T11 rubric, 'rollback exits 0' is insufficient — the plan must assert what the persisted state looks like after a rollback. Specifically: tickets that received a TICKET_DIGEST event before rollback will have the 'digest' key baked into their compiled_state (via SNAPSHOT); an older binary replaying those SNAPSHOTs restores the key verbatim (process_snapshot copies all compiled_state keys unconditionally). The plan does not acknowledge this intermediate state, does not assert it is safe, and does not describe a back-out path if the digest field turns out to be harmful to older clients.
findings.location: Scope section ('IN scope: event, reducer projection') and AC4 ('Older clients preserve-and-ignore the new event type')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which sub-fields of the digest record are public (shown in `show`) vs internal-only (available to retrieval but stripped from public output), and document whether `public_state` in `_present.py` needs a new stripping rule.
findings.finding: The plan says '`show` renders the digest presence + freshness' but does not specify which sub-fields of the stored digest record are public (shown) vs internal/stripped. The `public_state` filter in `_present.py` has explicit stripping rules for nested sensitive fields (e.g. HMAC hex under `attestations.<kind>.signature`). The new digest record will analogously contain a raw digest text (the LLM artifact) that may need filtering from public output — yet the plan provides no filtering spec.
findings.location: Scope section: '`show` renders the digest presence + freshness; the raw digest is available to the retrieval code'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` (the `EVENT_TYPES` allow-list and its error-message literal) to the plan's edit-target list for the new event type.
findings.finding: The write-path event-type allow-list at `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` (`EVENT_TYPES` frozenset + the error-message literal) is an unenumerated consumer. Any new event type must be added there before `stage_and_commit` will accept it; the plan does not name this file.
findings.location: Scope section / What section — new derived-record event type

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add or update an ADR (or extend ADR 0009 with a new section) to record the 'content-hash-keyed shared cache for billable LLM artifacts' contract so future artifact types can follow the same pattern.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new architectural sub-pattern — 'shared billable-artifact cache keyed by content-hash' — as a first-class design principle, but names no ADR creation or update to capture it. ADR 0009 covers the attestations validity-on-read mechanic; it does not address the 'never recompute a billable LLM artifact across clients' contract now being established here.
findings.location: ## Why / ## What — plan-level architectural framing
findings.suggested_fix: Either add a brief addendum to ADR 0009 covering the digest-cache extension, or create ADR 00XX: 'Shared content-hash-keyed cache for billable LLM artifacts' with: motivation (avoid recompute across clients), key design (content-hash self-invalidation, auto-push path, latest-wins semantics), and pointer to this plan as the reference implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a testing plan covering: (a) happy-path round-trip (write event → project → read freshness=current), (b) staleness detection (content changes → freshness=stale), (c) idempotent overwrite (second write for same ticket replaces prior record), and (d) hash normalization edge cases (empty description, no comments).
findings.finding: The plan introduces non-trivial new logic (content-hash computation over normalized fields, reducer projection, freshness-on-read comparison, idempotent overwrite) but contains no testing section, no test strategy, and no mention of how any of these behaviors will be verified — not even a pointer to an existing test harness.
findings.location: Entire plan — absence of any testing language

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command (e.g., `pytest` filter, `rebar show <ticket-id> | jq -e '.digest.content_hash'`, or equivalent) for each of the five ACs so that done-ness is independently reproducible.
findings.finding: The plan contains zero proving commands across all five ACs — no 'run X' command verifies any claim. Every AC describes a desired end-state but provides no concrete, executable check (e.g., a test invocation, a CLI command, a structured-output assertion) that would produce evidence of success.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all five bullets)
findings.suggested_fix:
For each AC, append a parenthetical proving command, e.g.:
- AC1: `pytest tests/reducer/test_digest_projection.py -k 'test_digest_written'`
- AC2: `rebar show <ticket-id> | jq -e '.digest | {present: (.content_hash != null), current: (.content_hash == .<current_hash>)}'`
- AC3: `git -C <repo> log --oneline -1` after event emission to confirm auto-commit; or a test that asserts the commit exists.
- AC4: run `rebar fsck` with an older binary against a store containing the new event type and assert WARN in output.
- AC5: emit two digest events for the same ticket and assert `jq '.digest | length' == 1` (single record, latest wins).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the content-hash input as a named, versioned constant or schema (e.g., `DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS_V1`) in the implementation spec, and note the invalidation consequence of changing it so future maintainers can plan accordingly.
findings.finding: The set of fields defining the content-hash ('title + description + comments, normalized') is stated inline in the plan with no mention of a named constant, schema, or configurability. If this field set changes in the future, all existing cached digests silently become stale, with no migration or versioning strategy noted.
findings.location: ## What — 'Content-hash helper over the exact fields fed to enrichment (title + description + comments, normalized)'
findings.suggested_fix: Name the helper (e.g., `digest_content_hash_v1(ticket)`) with an explicit docstring listing the included fields and normalization rules; note that changing either is a breaking cache change requiring a coordinated re-enrichment plan.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback for freshness-on-read when the current content-hash cannot be recomputed (e.g. fail-closed: treat as stale/unknown, never as current).
findings.finding: No fallback is specified for hash recomputation failure at read time. The plan says freshness is 'computed on read from the stored content-hash vs the ticket's current content-hash,' but does not specify what happens if the current hash cannot be computed (e.g. ticket state is partially readable). The attestation analogue (`compute_validity` / `current_material_fingerprint`) has an explicit fail-closed contract ('Returns None on any read error'), but the plan is silent on this.
findings.location: Scope section: 'freshness-on-read' / AC-2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope or AC-2: 'If the current content-hash cannot be recomputed (any read error), the digest is reported as present-but-stale (fail-closed); it is never reported as current.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact event type name string AND which registration set it joins (`KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` for reducer-projected+compaction-absorbed, or a new category), resolving the AC-1/AC-4 tension: if the digest is projected into reduced state it must be `KNOWN`, but then older clients don't `WARN`+preserve-and-ignore in the way AC-4 describes.
findings.finding: The new event type is never named, and the plan does not specify whether it belongs in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES (reducer-projected, compaction-absorbed into SNAPSHOT) or _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES (sidecar, preserved-and-ignored). These are mutually exclusive registration paths with different compaction semantics: KNOWN types are squashed into SNAPSHOT (compiled_state), while NON_REPLAY types are preserved as raw files. AC-1 ('projected into reduced state') implies KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES, but AC-4 ('preserved-and-ignored by older clients') is the behavior of an UNKNOWN type — a structural contradiction the plan leaves unresolved.
findings.location: Scope section / AC-1 / AC-4
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (A) add the new type to KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES (reducer-projected, compaction-absorbed) and update AC-4 to say 'older clients skip the new processor and the event is silently absorbed by compaction'; or (B) model it like REVIEW_RESULT (add to _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES, NOT reducer-projected) and instead compute the digest projection at read time from the preserved sidecar file — and update AC-1 accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/schemas/ticket_state.schema.json` to the plan's edit-target list to document the new digest field alongside the existing `attestations` definition.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/schemas/ticket_state.schema.json` is an unenumerated consumer. Adding a new top-level field (`digest` or equivalent map) to compiled ticket state requires updating this schema; the plan does not name it.
findings.location: Scope section — 'reducer projection into ticket state' / AC1 'projected into reduced state'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback section stating whether reverting the binary is sufficient (existing digest events are ignored by older clients and therefore safe), or whether event-scrubbing/migration is needed, and how coordinated rollback with digest consumers (Stage 3) should be sequenced.
findings.finding: The plan specifies backward-compatible event consumption (older clients preserve-and-ignore) but never states an explicit rollback strategy: what operators do if a bug is found post-deploy, whether already-committed digest events are benign under a binary rollback, and whether any data-cleanup step is needed.
findings.location: Scope section / AC item 4 ('Older clients preserve-and-ignore the new event type')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph rollback note: 'Rolling back to a pre-digest binary is safe because older clients already preserve-and-ignore the new event type; no event-scrubbing is required. If the reducer logic is found to be wrong, re-emitting corrected digest events (idempotent latest-wins) is the forward-fix path. Coordinated rollback with Stage 3 is only required if Stage 3 has already shipped a hard dependency on the digest record.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the RETIRE path: specify whether digest records are deleted when their parent ticket is deleted/archived (e.g., a tombstone event, a gc pass, or an explicit 'accumulate-and-ignore' policy decision).
findings.finding: No RETIRE/cleanup path is defined for digest records when a ticket is deleted or archived; the shared store will accumulate stale digest records indefinitely.
findings.location: Scope section / AC3 ('shared via the normal auto-commit/auto-push path')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to Scope or a new AC: 'Digest records for deleted tickets are either removed by a compensating event emitted during ticket deletion, or are explicitly accepted as benign orphans (e.g., ignored by fsck after ticket-not-found).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the concurrency/atomicity guarantee for concurrent digest events: confirm that identical content produces identical events (naturally idempotent) and that for differing content the reducer's append-order latest-wins is the intended and sufficient tie-break.
findings.finding: Concurrent enrichment by two clients for the same ticket — both emitting a digest event simultaneously — is not addressed; the plan does not confirm that the event-sourced ordering guarantee makes this safe and idempotent.
findings.location: AC5 ('A re-enrichment overwrites the prior digest for that ticket (idempotent, latest-wins)')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC with a concrete validation mechanism for the cache-hit value: e.g., 'given a ticket whose digest is already stored at the current content-hash, calling enrichment does NOT issue an LLM request (verifiable via log/metric/test double count)'.
findings.finding: No AC carries a concrete validation mechanism: all five ACs are internal system-state checks (event written, projection keyed, flag set, fsck warns, idempotent overwrite) with no before/after workflow comparison, operational metric, or dogfooding step that would let a reviewer confirm the cache actually avoids a redundant LLM call in practice.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all five items)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'Given a digest already stored at the ticket's current content-hash, a second enrichment attempt returns the cached digest without calling the LLM (verifiable in tests via a call-count assertion on the LLM client).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the 'content-hash helper' is NOT a new wrapper function but rather a direct call to the existing `rebar._store.canonical.content_hash` primitive (cite it explicitly in the plan to prevent a second implementation).
findings.finding: The plan calls for a 'content-hash helper over the exact fields fed to enrichment (title + description + comments, normalized)' as if it is a new abstraction. However, `rebar._store.canonical.content_hash` is already the designated single seam for this (4+ call-sites: `conflict_resolver.py`, `mutation.py`, `workflow/schema.py`, `llm/workflow/__init__.py`). If the plan intends to introduce a new named wrapper function (e.g. `digest_content_hash(state)`), that wrapper will have only one call-site at launch — a premature abstraction. If it intends to inline `content_hash(...)` from the canonical module directly, no finding; but the plan's language ('content-hash helper') implies a new function.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Content-hash helper over the exact fields fed to enrichment (title + description + comments, normalized)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Content-hash helper over the exact fields fed to enrichment' with 'Content-hash computed via `rebar._store.canonical.content_hash` over `{"title": ..., "description": ..., "comments": [...]}` (normalized/sorted) — no new helper function needed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rationale for why the digest content-hash field set (title + description + comments) differs from `material_fingerprint` (description + file_impact + children), confirming this is intentional and not a rebuild of the existing fingerprint.
findings.finding: The plan's content-hash input set (title + description + comments) overlaps with but diverges from the existing `material_fingerprint` (description + file_impact + children, in `pass1.py:551`). The plan gives no rationale for the different field set. If enrichment actually consumes the same fields as `material_fingerprint`, the plan would be building a second hash of nearly the same content rather than reusing the established fingerprint. If the fields genuinely differ (e.g. comments are included for digest but not for plan-review material), the plan should state this explicitly so reviewers can verify it's not an accidental divergence.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Content-hash helper over the exact fields fed to enrichment (title + description + comments, normalized)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `_processors.py` line citation from `:395` (tag-processing code) to the actual attestations machinery: `process_signature` at line 456 / `attestation_kind` at line 428.
findings.finding: The plan cites `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:395` as attestations machinery to mirror, but line 395 is `state["tags"] = []` inside the `process_edit` tags-handling branch — not attestation code. The actual attestations processor (`process_signature`, `attestation_kind`) lives at lines 428–496. An implementer following this line citation to find the pattern will land in the wrong function.
findings.location: What section, sentence: 'src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:395'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:395` with `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:428` (attestation_kind) and `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:456` (process_signature) in the What section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `_processors.py` line reference from `:395` (process_edit tags branch) to the actual attestation processor location (`process_signature`, ~line 456) so implementers anchor on the right structural model.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:395` is cited as part of the attestations machinery to mirror, but line 395 is actually the `state['tags'] = []` branch inside `process_edit` — not attestations code. The attestations/SIGNATURE processor begins at line 456 (`process_signature`). The plan's line-level anchor is incorrect.
findings.location: What section — 'mirroring the attestations machinery (`src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:395`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:395` to `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:456` (the `process_signature` function that projects into `state['attestations']`).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
